macro_segments.title: Introduction to Tumah Hutrah vs. Tumah Dechuya in the Context of Korban Tzibur and Pikuach Nefesh

micro_segments.title: The fundamental debate: Is tumah permitted or overridden for communal offerings
Connection to pikuach nefesh on Shabbat and the Kohen Gadol on Yom Kippur
Distinguishing between tumah from outside and tumah from personal status; the scope of the debate
Parallel question: Does performing melachot on Shabbat for korban tzibur represent hutra or dechuya

macro_segments.title: The Debate of Rav Nachman and Rav Sheshet: Hutra vs. Dechuya with Extreme Formulations

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's position: hutra—even with tahor and tamei Kohanim present, tamei may bring the tamid
Rav Sheshet's source: the Braita of minchat ha-omer becoming tamei; preference for tahor
The curious instruction: 'be quiet and do not announce it' (hevei pikeach veshitok)
Rav Nachman's concession: hutra only applies when sheyirayim (remnants) are eaten
Second parallel: minchat nesachim becoming tamei; same preference and instruction pattern

macro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's Response: Distinguishing Fixed-Time from Non-Fixed-Time Korbanot

micro_segments.title: The bull of avodah zarah: a korban tzibur without fixed time, hence not overriding tumah
Aaron's ram on Yom Kippur: a yachid with fixed time, overriding tumah but preferred tahor
The sheep brought with the omer: returning to the omer discussion
The core principle: fixed time overrides tumah and Shabbat, not tzibur status alone

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Resolution: Ritzuy Tzitz for Non-Fixed-Time Korbanot When the Object is Tamei

micro_segments.title: When the person is tamei: only fixed-time korbanot override; otherwise invalid
When the object (korban) is tamei: ritzuy tzitz allows validity even for non-fixed-time
The principle of 'keep it quiet': acknowledging the impropriety while allowing bedieved validity

macro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's Response: Distinguishing Fixed-Time from Non-Fixed-Time Korbanot (continued)

micro_segments.title: Chart of korban categories: fixed-time tzibur, fixed-time yachid, non-fixed tzibur, non-fixed yachid

macro_segments.title: Blood and Meat Becoming Tamei: Braita on Validity of Tamei Sacrifices and the Role of Tzitz

micro_segments.title: Case: blood of an ola becoming tamei, thrown unintentionally; outcome differs if intentional
Introduction of ritzuy tzitz principle: only works when objects are tamei, not when people are tamei
Braita on tzitz: applies to blood, meat, fat of korbanot whether yachid or tzibur, intentional or unintentional
Why does the braita mention korban tzibur when it already has hutrah? Rav Nachman's answer: non-fixed-time tzibur needs tzitz

macro_segments.title: Elimination of Alternatives: Why Tzitz Works Only for Tumah, Not for Pigul or Notar

micro_segments.title: Pigul: korban brought with intent to eat at wrong time; explicitly disqualified, tzitz cannot help
Notar: leftover meat; occurs after validity, so tzitz need not apply
Logical conclusion: tumah is the only category of invalidity where tzitz can functionally apply
Braita rationale: tumah is sometimes permitted (hutrah) in tzibur, making it a plausible category for tzitz to address

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Framework: Consensus on Broken Tzitz; Debate on Tzitz Hanging in Locker

micro_segments.title: Implication of positions: Yehuda (must be worn) suggests hutra concept; Shimon (always works) suggests dechuya acknowledges function
If tzitz is broken: all agree it does not make korbanot acceptable
If tzitz is on its peg (in locker, not being worn): is it functioning? debate between Yehuda and Shimon

macro_segments.title: Debate on Whether Tzitz Must Be Worn: Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon on the Forehead Requirement

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda: tzitz makes korbanot acceptable only while worn on the forehead (juxtaposition of al mitzcho and venasa)
Rabbi Shimon: tzitz makes korbanot acceptable always (tamid leratzon) regardless of whether worn
Rabbi Yehuda's interpretation of tamid: constant consciousness when wearing, not constant wearing
Comparison to tefillin: obligation to be constantly aware of tefillin while wearing them
Rabbi Shimon's counterpoint: broken tzitz cannot function; emphasis on fitness to be worn

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon (on tzitz wearing)
Tumas Meis (impurity from contact with dead)
